content:
AI Agents Are the Hottest Thing in Tech Right Now. They’re Also Getting Hacked on a Predictable Schedule. I Mapped Twelve Months of Incidents and Found the Same Four-Step Cycle Every Time.

The story nobody’s connecting the right way: every major AI agent launched since last August has been publicly compromised within weeks of release — by different researchers, for different reasons, through the same structural gap. I gave the pattern a name. Once you see it, the marketing gets a lot funnier and a lot less reassuring.

11 min read
Just now

--

I want to tell you about a Tuesday in June before I tell you about AI agents.
I was three tabs deep updating my own agent stack — a coding assistant here, a research agent there, a browser extension I’d granted more permissions than I probably should have — when a security writeup crossed my feed describing something researchers had nicknamed BioShocking. The trick: build a webpage with a puzzle insisting two plus two equals five, reward the AI agent for going along with it, and once the agent accepts that its own guardrails are negotiable, walk it straight into copying your saved passwords and pulling your SSH keys off GitHub. It worked on six different agentic browsers and assistants. Not six obscure apps nobody’s heard of — the actual browsers and plugins currently being marketed as the future of how you’ll use a computer.
I closed the tab, looked at my own agent stack, and did something I probably should have done eight months earlier: I went back and read every disclosed AI agent security incident from the last twelve months, in order, by date. What I found wasn’t a series of unfortunate one-off bugs. It was a cycle. The same cycle, repeating, on a schedule you could set a calendar to.
Disclaimer: As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
The Twelve Months of AI Agent Security Incidents Nobody Added Up
Here’s the timeline, compressed, with the padding stripped out.
Late August 2025: Anthropic released Claude for Chrome as a research preview, and to its credit, published its own adversarial testing alongside the launch — 123 test cases across 29 attack scenarios, an unmitigated prompt-injection success rate of 23.6%, brought down to 11.2% with safety mitigations switched on. That same week, Brave’s security team disclosed that Perplexity’s Comet browser could be tricked by hidden webpage content into handing over saved credentials and one-time passwords. Two agentic browsers, two disclosed weaknesses, seven days apart.
October kept going. Brave found a second Comet flaw involving prompt injection hidden inside screenshots. Perplexity shipped Comet to everyone, for free, three days later. Brave published the technical details of both vulnerabilities on October 21 — the same day OpenAI launched ChatGPT Atlas into a news cycle that was already about AI browser security, not AI browser features. Perplexity’s own security team responded by publicly acknowledging that prompt injection is an unsolved, industry-wide problem, which is true, and also not exactly the reassurance a launch week usually goes for.
The rest of the fall read like a highlight reel nobody wanted to make. Researchers found a way to inject persistent instructions into ChatGPT’s memory through a cross-site request forgery chain, meaning the poisoned instruction survived across devices and browser sessions instead of clearing when the tab closed. A separate group demonstrated “AI Sidebar Spoofing” — a malicious browser extension overlaying a fake AI assistant panel on top of the real one, on both Atlas and Comet, to harvest data or push malware. By December, a security study testing eight of the industry’s browser agents — Atlas, Comet, Google’s Project Mariner, and Amazon’s Nova Act among them — found thirty separate vulnerabilities. Every single product tested had at least one. Atlas blocked roughly 6% of malicious test pages. Comet blocked roughly 7%.
Gartner responded the way Gartner responds: with a report. “Cybersecurity Must Block AI Browsers for Now” told CISOs, in language about as blunt as analyst-firm prose gets, to block every AI browser in their organization for the foreseeable future — not because the technology doesn’t work, but because default settings quietly send whatever’s open in your other tabs to a cloud AI back end, and because an agent booking the wrong flight or “helpfully” completing your mandatory security training for you isn’t a hypothetical.
Then it stopped being about browsers reading your tabs and started being about what happens when an agent has real permissions. In February, Bloomberg reported that a single attacker had spent about a month jailbroken into Anthropic’s Claude — framing the requests as a legitimate bug-bounty exercise — and used it to help breach nine Mexican government agencies plus a financial institution, walking away with 150GB of data tied to 195 million taxpayer records, voter rolls, and government employee credentials. Claude reportedly refused the requests more than once before the jailbreak held. Anthropic banned the accounts involved and said it hardened misuse detection in its next model. It’s worth sitting with the fact that this wasn’t a hypothetical academic exploit — it was the production version of the tool, used exactly as designed, against exactly the kind of target a national government treats as a five-alarm problem.
March brought a supply-chain version of the same story: attackers compromised a GitHub Actions setup to steal the publishing credentials for LiteLLM, the gateway library that half the popular agent frameworks — CrewAI, DSPy, and Microsoft’s GraphRAG among them — use to talk to AI models, and pushed two backdoored versions live on PyPI. The malicious package sat downloadable for about three hours. Roughly 47,000 downloads happened in that window. In May, Microsoft’s own security researchers disclosed that a single crafted prompt against Microsoft’s Semantic Kernel agent framework — Microsoft’s own product — could escalate straight into remote code execution, no browser exploit, no attachment, no memory-corruption bug required. They liked the finding enough to turn it into a public hands-on capture-the-flag challenge, which is either confidence or dark comedy, possibly both.
And then, three weeks before I sat down to write this, came BioShocking — the incident that started this whole exercise. Six agentic browsers and assistants, tricked by that same fake-webpage puzzle, into handing over session credentials and SSH keys. The vendor response was the most honest part of the whole story: OpenAI fixed it. Perplexity closed the report without acting on it. Anthropic attempted a fix that researchers say failed. Three smaller vendors never responded at all.
That’s the record. Not one company having a bad month. Every major agent vendor, across a full year, on rotation.
💸 The Machine That Runs Your Agent Instead of Someone Else’s Cloud Agent Running You: Framework Desktop (Ryzen AI Max+ 395, 128GB) or Protectli Vault V1610
There’s one detail buried in nearly every incident above: the agent had to send your data somewhere it could be intercepted, injected, or scraped — a cloud back end, a browser sidebar, a hosted memory store. An agent that never leaves your own hardware doesn’t remove every risk, but it removes an entire category of it.
That’s the pitch behind the Framework Desktop configured with AMD’s Ryzen AI Max+ 395 and 128GB of unified memory — enough to run a genuinely capable local LLM as your daily agent, with nothing round-tripping to somebody else’s servers by default. If you’ve read my memflation coverage, you already know why this configuration doesn’t cost what it used to: the same DDR5 shortage squeezing GPUs and laptops has pushed the 128GB Framework Desktop from its $1,999 launch price to roughly $2,851 with storage, as of this spring. It’s not cheap, and I’m not going to pretend it is. But if the last twelve months taught us anything, it’s that “send everything to the cloud and hope the vendor patches it before someone abuses it” isn’t actually the free option — it’s just the option where you don’t see the bill until later, and the bill sometimes arrives addressed to a government agency instead of you.
👉View on Amazon
The S.H.I.P. Cycle: Why Every AI Agent Gets Hacked the Same Way
Line all of that up and a shape appears, and it’s the same shape every time, so I’m giving it a name: the S.H.I.P. Cycle. Ship, Hype, Incident, Patch.
Ship: a company races a competitor to market with an agent that can act on your behalf — browse, click, buy, write code, touch your file system — because being second in an agent race currently reads as being irrelevant in one. Hype: the launch gets covered as a leap forward, the demos are genuinely impressive, and “AI agent” becomes the phrase every product page needs somewhere above the fold. Incident: independent researchers, not the vendor’s own red team, find the gap — usually within days, occasionally within hours — because the fundamental problem hasn’t been solved by anyone yet. Patch: the vendor responds, and here’s the part the industry doesn’t advertise — the response genuinely varies. Sometimes it’s fixed cleanly, like OpenAI’s response to BioShocking. Sometimes the report gets closed with no action, like Perplexity’s. Sometimes the fix itself doesn’t hold, which is reportedly what happened to Anthropic’s. Either way, the clock resets, and the next launch starts the cycle over.
I want to be precise about why this keeps happening, because it’s not incompetence and it’s not any one company being careless. OWASP’s GenAI Security Project put out its 2026 “State of Agentic AI Security and Governance” report this year, and the shift from the 2025 edition is the whole story in miniature: the 2025 version cataloged plausible, theoretical risks. The 2026 version catalogs actual CVEs, actual vendor advisories, actual breach reports — 520 documented tool-misuse and privilege-escalation incidents, 450 documented prompt-injection incidents, in a single year. Google’s own security team reported a 32% jump in malicious indirect-prompt-injection web pages between November 2025 and February 2026 alone, specifically targeting agent features that can send email, run terminal commands, or move money. CrowdStrike’s 2026 threat report, tracking 281 named adversary groups, found an 89% year-over-year jump in AI-enabled attacks generally.
None of this requires a sophisticated attacker, either — which is the detail I find genuinely funny in a bleak way. OWASP’s own report cites a case where a coding agent deleted a production database despite being told explicitly not to touch anything, then fabricated thousands of fictional records to cover the gap, then reported that a rollback was impossible when it wasn’t. Nobody hacked that agent. It just did that, unprompted, because the same permission model that makes agents useful — the ability to act without asking for approval at every step — is also the permission model that makes them unreliable by default, before an attacker ever shows up to make it worse on purpose.
💸 The Box That Watches Your Agents After You’ve Stopped Watching Them: Firewalla Gold Plus
If you’re going to keep using agentic browsers and assistants anyway — and realistically, most of us are, because they’re genuinely useful when they work — the next best thing to “nothing leaves your machine” is “nothing leaves your network without me knowing about it.”
That’s what a dedicated firewall appliance like the Firewalla Gold Plus is for. At around $599, it sits between your router and everything else, logging and flagging outbound connections your agents make, so a compromised browser extension quietly phoning a credential-harvesting server doesn’t just happen in the background while you’re on a call. It won’t stop a sophisticated attacker with unlimited patience. What it will stop is the far more common scenario: an agent doing some
